name: mitgcmutils
documentation:
# MITgcmutils

Python utilities for the MIT General Circulation Model,
[MITgcm](http://mitgcm.org).

This package is developed as a part of MITgcm on
[github](https://github.com/MITgcm/MITgcm/tree/master/utils/python/MITgcmutils).

It is documented in the MITgcm
[manual](http://mitgcm.rtfd.io/en/latest/utilities/utilities.html#mitgcmutils).

functions.name: MITgcmutils.ptracers.iolabel
documentation:
Map tracer number (1..3843) to 2-character I/O label:

| 1..99      =>  01..99
| 100..619   =>  0a..0Z,1a..9Z
| 620..3843  =>  aa..ZZ

Parameters
----------
i : int
    ptracer number (1..3843)

Returns
-------
string
    2-character I/O label

functions.name: MITgcmutils.ptracers.iolabel2num
documentation: Map 2-character IO label to tracer number, the inverse of iolabel()

functions.name: MITgcmutils.diagnostics.readstats
documentation:
statsPerLayer,statsVertInt,itrs = readstats(fname)

Read a diagstats text file into record arrays (or dictionaries).

Parameters
----------
fname : string
    name of diagstats file to read

Returns
-------
statsPerLayer : record array or dict of arrays
    statistics per layer, shape (len(itrs), len(nReg), Nr, 5)
statsVertInt  : record array or dict of arrays
    column integrals, shape (len(itrs), len(nReg), 5)
itrs : list of int
    iteration numbers found in the file

Notes
-----
- The 5 columns of the resulting arrays are
  average, std.dev, min, max and total volume.
- There is a record (or dictionary key) for each field found in the file.
- Regional axis is omitted if nReg == 1

modules.name: MITgcmutils.utils
documentation: Created by EGavilan Pascual-Ahuir on 2023-04-07

functions.name: MITgcmutils.utils.gen_blanklist
documentation:
Computes blanklist for data.exch2

# Parameters

depth

: depth [m].

sNx

: x point in each tile.

sNy

: y point in each tile.

tilemap

: True : output tile contourplot,
  default False.

# Returns

blank

: empty tiles numbers

fig(optional)

: tile plot

# Usage

```pycon
>>> blank=gen_blanklist(bathy, 5, 5, tilemap=False)
10,11,12,..,103
>>> [blank,fig]=gen_blanklist(bathy, 5, 5, tilemap=True)
10,11,12,..,103
```

# Example

```pycon
>>> eg_blanklist()
10,11,12,..,103
```

functions.name: MITgcmutils.utils.hfac
documentation:
Computes hFacC,W,S

# Parameters

depth

: Depth [m].

rF

: Depth at the f point.

hFacMin

: Min fraction for partial vertical levels.

hFacMinDr

: Min depth for partial vertical levels.

htype

: Types of hfac: one or more of 'C','S','W', default='C'.

# Returns

hFacC,W,S

: The hfac arrays requested in htype.

# Usage

```pycon
>>> [hFacC]=mit.hfac(depth,rF,0.3,50,'C')
```

# Example

```pycon
>>> [hFacC]=eg_hfac()
```

# Notes

The first row and column are filled with zeros
for the hFacS and hFacW, respectively.

functions.name: MITgcmutils.utils.readbin
documentation:
Read meta-data files as written by MITgcm.

# Parameters

fname

: name of file to read

ndims

: dimension of the file

dataprec

: precision of resulting file ('float32' or 'float64')

machineformat

: endianness ('b' or 'l', default 'b')

# Returns

arr

: numpy array of the data read

# Usage

```pycon
>>> arr=readbin('bathy.bin',[Y,X])
```

functions.name: MITgcmutils.utils.tilecmap
documentation:
Pseudocolor plot of land mask with tiles superimposed, optionally
showing the values of arr for a single tile.

# Parameters

arr

: values to plot, land mask is taken as arr==fill_value

sNx

: number of x points in each tile

sNy

: number of y points in each tile

tilen

: plot a specific tile, default None

sel_zoom

: zooming range, default 5

fill_value

: default 0

# Returns

figure

: Plot of land mask, tiles and arr values

# Usage

```pycon
>>> [fig]=tilecmap(bathy, 5, 5)
>>> [fig]=tilecmap(bathy, 5, 5, 66, sel_zoom=4)
```

# Example

```pycon
>>> eg_tilemap()
```

functions.name: MITgcmutils.utils.writebin
documentation:
Write an array to a bin format for MITgcm

# Parameters

fbase

: Name of file to write

arr

: Numpy array to write

dataprec

: precision of resulting file ('float32' or 'float64')
  ('float32' by default)

machineformat

: 'b' or 'l' for big or little endian
  ('b' by default)

# Usage

```pycon
>>> writebin('data.bin',arr)
```

p  : pressure [dBar]

functions.name: MITgcmutils.density.linear
documentation:
Computes in-situ density of water

Density of water using the linear EOS of McDougall (1987).

# Parameters

salt

: salinity [psu (PSS-78)]

theta

: potential temperature [degree C (IPTS-68)]
  same shape as salt

sref

: default 30 [psu (PSS-78)]

tref

: default 20 [degree C (IPTS-68)]

sbeta

: default 7.4e-04 [1/C]

talpha

: default 2.0e-04 [(g/Kg)-1]

rhonil

: default 999.8 [(g/Kg)-1];

# Returns

dens

: density [kg/m^3]

# Example

```pycon
>>> dens.linear(35.5, 3.)
1007.268506
```

# Notes

- Source code written by Martin Losch 2002-08-09
- Converted to python by Gavilan on 2024-07-18

functions.name: MITgcmutils.density.poly3
documentation:
Calculates in-situ density as approximated by the POLY3 method
based on the Knudsen formula (see Bryan and Cox 1972).

# Parameters

poly3

: 'POLY3.COEFFS' using INI_POLY3

salt

: salinity [psu (PSS-78)]

theta

: potential temperature [degree C (IPTS-68)];
  same shape as salt

# Returns

dens

: density [kg/m^3]

# Example

```pycon
>>> p=ini_poly3()
>>> T=rdmds('T',100)
>>> S=rdmds('S',100)
>>> D=poly3(p,salt,theta)
>>> or to work within a single model level
>>> D=poly3(P[3,:],S[3,:,:],T[3,:,:])
```

# Notes

- Source code written by Martin Losch 2002-08-09
- Converted to python by Gavilan on 2024-07-18

functions.name: MITgcmutils.density.ini_poly3
documentation:
Reads the file fpath (default 'POLY3.COEFFS') and returns
coefficients in poly

functions.name: MITgcmutils.density.jmd95
documentation:
Computes in-situ density of sea water

Density of Sea Water using Jackett and McDougall 1995 (JAOT 12)
polynomial (modified UNESCO polynomial).

# Parameters

salt

: salinity [psu (PSS-78)]

theta

: potential temperature [degree C (IPTS-68)];
  same shape as s

p

: sea pressure [dbar]. p may have dims 1x1,
  mx1, 1xn or mxn for s(mxn)

# Returns

dens

: density [kg/m^3]

# Example

```pycon
>>> dens.jmd95(35.5, 3., 3000.)
1041.83267
```

# Notes

- Jackett and McDougall, 1995, JAOT 12(4), pp. 381-388
- Source code written by Martin Losch 2002-08-09
- Converted to python by jahn on 2010-04-29

functions.name: MITgcmutils.density.bulkmodjmd95
documentation: Compute bulk modulus

functions.name: MITgcmutils.density.unesco
documentation:
Computes in-situ density of sea water

Density of Sea Water using Fofonoff and Millard (1983)
polynomial.

# Parameters

salt

: salinity [psu (PSS-78)]

theta

: potential temperature [degree C (IPTS-68)];
  same shape as s

p

: sea pressure [dbar]. p may have dims 1x1,
  mx1, 1xn or mxn for s(mxn)

# Returns

dens

: density [kg/m^3]

# Example

```pycon
>>> dens.unesco(35.5, 3., 3000.)
1041.87663
```

# Notes

- Source code written by Martin Losch 2002-08-09
- Converted to python by Gavilan on 2024-07-18

functions.name: MITgcmutils.density.bulkmodunesco
documentation: Compute bulk modulus

functions.name: MITgcmutils.density.mdjwf
documentation:
Computes in-situ density of sea water

Density of Sea Water using the McDougall et al. 2003 (JAOT 20)
polynomial.

# Parameters

salt

: salinity [psu (PSS-78)]

theta

: potential temperature [degree C (IPTS-68)];
  same shape as salt

p

: sea pressure [dbar]. p may have dims 1x1,
  mx1, 1xn or mxn for salt(mxn)

# Returns

dens

: density [kg/m^3]

# Example

```pycon
>>> dens.mdjwf(35.5, 3., 3000.)
1041.83305
```

# Notes

- McDougall et al., 2003, JAOT 20(5), pp. 730-741
- Converted to python by Gavilan on 2024-07-18

functions.name: MITgcmutils.density.teos10
documentation:
Computes in-situ density of sea water

Density of Sea Water using TEOS-10.

# Parameters

salt

: absolute salinity [g/kg]

theta

: conservative temperature [degree C (IPTS-68)];
  same shape as s

p

: sea pressure [dbar]. p may have dims 1x1,
  mx1, 1xn or mxn for s(mxn)

# Returns

dens

: density [kg/m^3]

# Example

```pycon
>>> dens.teos10(35.5, 3., 3000.)
1041.70578
```

# Notes

- Converted to python by Gavilan on 2024-07-18

modules.name: MITgcmutils.conversion
documentation: created EGavilan Pascual-Ahuir on 2023-04-07

functions.name: MITgcmutils.conversion.pfromz
documentation:
Computes pressure (dbar) of sea water from depth.

# Parameters

rC

: Depth at c point [m].

rF0

: Depth at rF[k=0], default 0.

lat

: Latitude to compute unesco gravity. If None, use gravity = 9.81.
  Default None.

rhoConst

: Density of seawater, default 1027.5.

eosRefP0

: EOS reference pressure (Pa), default 1.01325e+5.

top_Pres

: Reference pressure at the top, default 0.

surf_pRef

: Surface pressure (Pa), default 1.01325e+5.

# Returns

p

: Pressure [dbar]. If rC and lat are both 1-dimensional, a 2-dimensional
  array corresponding to the outer product will be returned.

# Example

```pycon
>>> pfromz(-1000)
1007.9775
>>> pfromz([-100,-1000])
100.7978, 1007.9775
>>> pfromz([-100,-1000],lat=90)
101.0256, 1010.2562
>>> pfromz(-1000,lat=[70,90])
1009.6304, 1010.2562
```

functions.name: MITgcmutils.cs.pcol.pcol
documentation:
Plots 2D scalar fields on the MITgcm cubed sphere grid with pcolormesh.

# Parameters

x

: 'xg', that is, x coordinate of the points one half grid cell to the
  left and bottom, that is vorticity points for tracers, etc.

y

: 'yg', that is, y coordinate of same points

data

: scalar field at tracer points

projection

: used to transform if present.
  Unfortunatly, cylindrical and conic maps are limited to
  the [-180 180] range.
  projection = 'sphere' results in a 3D visualization on the sphere
  without any specific projection. Good for debugging.

# Example

```pycon
>>> from mpl_toolkits.basemap import Basemap
>>> import MITgcmutils as mit
>>> import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
>>> from sq import sq
>>>
>>> x=mit.rdmds('XG'); y=mit.rdmds('YG'); e=mit.rdmds('Eta',np.inf)
>>> fig = plt.figure();
>>> mp = Basemap(projection='moll',lon_0 = 0.,
>>>              resolution = 'l', area_thresh = 1000.)
>>> plt.clf()
>>> h = mit.cs.pcol(x,y,sq(e), projection = mp)
>>> mp.fillcontinents(color = 'grey')
>>> mp.drawmapboundary()
>>> mp.drawmeridians(np.arange(0, 360, 30))
>>> mp.drawparallels(np.arange(-90, 90, 30))
>>> plt.show()
```

modules.name: MITgcmutils.netcdf
documentation:
NetCDF reader/writer module.

This module is used to read and create NetCDF files. NetCDF files are
accessed through the `netcdf_file` object. Data written to and from NetCDF
files are contained in `netcdf_variable` objects. Attributes are given
as member variables of the `netcdf_file` and `netcdf_variable` objects.

This module implements the Scientific.IO.NetCDF API to read and create
NetCDF files. The same API is also used in the PyNIO and pynetcdf
modules, allowing these modules to be used interchangeably when working
with NetCDF files.

classes.name: MITgcmutils.netcdf.netcdf_file

methods.name: MITgcmutils.netcdf.netcdf_file.__init__
documentation: Initialize netcdf_file from fileobj (str or file-like).

methods.name: MITgcmutils.netcdf.netcdf_file.close
documentation: Closes the NetCDF file.

methods.name: MITgcmutils.netcdf.netcdf_file.createDimension
documentation:
Adds a dimension to the Dimension section of the NetCDF data structure.

Note that this function merely adds a new dimension that the variables can
reference.  The values for the dimension, if desired, should be added as
a variable using `createVariable`, referring to this dimension.

Parameters
----------
name : str
    Name of the dimension (Eg, 'lat' or 'time').
length : int
    Length of the dimension.

See Also
--------
createVariable

methods.name: MITgcmutils.netcdf.netcdf_file.createVariable
documentation:
Create an empty variable for the `netcdf_file` object, specifying its data
type and the dimensions it uses.

# Parameters

name

: Name of the new variable.

type

: Data type of the variable.

dimensions

: List of the dimension names used by the variable, in the desired order.

# Returns

variable

: The newly created `netcdf_variable` object.
  This object has also been added to the `netcdf_file` object as well.

# See Also

createDimension

# Notes

Any dimensions to be used by the variable should already exist in the
NetCDF data structure or should be created by `createDimension` prior to
creating the NetCDF variable.

methods.name: MITgcmutils.netcdf.netcdf_file.flush
documentation:
Perform a sync-to-disk flush if the `netcdf_file` object is in write mode.

See Also
--------
sync : Identical function

A file object for NetCDF data.

A `netcdf_file` object has two standard attributes: `dimensions` and
`variables`. The values of both are dictionaries, mapping dimension
names to their associated lengths and variable names to variables,
respectively. Application programs should never modify these
dictionaries.

All other attributes correspond to global attributes defined in the
NetCDF file. Global file attributes are created by assigning to an
attribute of the `netcdf_file` object.

# Parameters

filename

: string -> filename

mode

: read-write mode, default is 'r'

mmap

: Whether to mmap `filename` when reading. Default is True
  when `filename` is a file name, False when `filename` is a
  file-like object

version

: version of netcdf to read / write, where 1 means *Classic
  format* and 2 means *64-bit offset format*. Default is 1. See
  [here](http://www.unidata.ucar.edu/software/netcdf/docs/netcdf/Which-Format.html)
  for more info.

# Notes

The major advantage of this module over other modules is that it doesn't
require the code to be linked to the NetCDF libraries. This module is
derived from [pupynere](https://bitbucket.org/robertodealmeida/pupynere/).

NetCDF files are a self-describing binary data format. The file contains
metadata that describes the dimensions and variables in the file. More
details about NetCDF files can be found [here](http://www.unidata.ucar.edu/software/netcdf/docs/netcdf.html). There
are three main sections to a NetCDF data structure:

1. Dimensions
2. Variables
3. Attributes

The dimensions section records the name and length of each dimension used
by the variables. The variables would then indicate which dimensions it
uses and any attributes such as data units, along with containing the data
values for the variable. It is good practice to include a
variable that is the same name as a dimension to provide the values for
that axes. Lastly, the attributes section would contain additional
information such as the name of the file creator or the instrument used to
collect the data.

When writing data to a NetCDF file, there is often the need to indicate the
'record dimension'. A record dimension is the unbounded dimension for a
variable. For example, a temperature variable may have dimensions of
latitude, longitude and time. If one wants to add more temperature data to
the NetCDF file as time progresses, then the temperature variable should
have the time dimension flagged as the record dimension.

In addition, the NetCDF file header contains the position of the data in
the file, so access can be done in an efficient manner without loading
unnecessary data into memory. It uses the `mmap` module to create
Numpy arrays mapped to the data on disk, for the same purpose.

# Examples

To create a NetCDF file:

> ```pycon
> >>> from scipy.io import netcdf
> >>> f = netcdf.netcdf_file('simple.nc', 'w')
> >>> f.history = 'Created for a test'
> >>> f.createDimension('time', 10)
> >>> time = f.createVariable('time', 'i', ('time',))
> >>> time[:] = np.arange(10)
> >>> time.units = 'days since 2008-01-01'
> >>> f.close()
> ```

Note the assignment of `range(10)` to `time[:]`. Exposing the slice
of the time variable allows for the data to be set in the object, rather
than letting `range(10)` overwrite the `time` variable.

To read the NetCDF file we just created:

> ```pycon
> >>> from scipy.io import netcdf
> >>> f = netcdf.netcdf_file('simple.nc', 'r')
> >>> print(f.history)
> Created for a test
> >>> time = f.variables['time']
> >>> print(time.units)
> days since 2008-01-01
> >>> print(time.shape)
> (10,)
> >>> print(time[-1])
> 9
> >>> f.close()
> ```

A NetCDF file can also be used as context manager:

> ```pycon
> >>> from scipy.io import netcdf
> >>> with netcdf.netcdf_file('simple.nc', 'r') as f:
> >>>     print(f.history)
> Created for a test
> ```

classes.name: MITgcmutils.netcdf.netcdf_variable

methods.name: MITgcmutils.netcdf.netcdf_variable.isrec
documentation:
Returns whether the variable has a record dimension or not.

A record dimension is a dimension along which additional data could be
easily appended in the netcdf data structure without much rewriting of
the data file. This attribute is a read-only property of the
`netcdf_variable`.

methods.name: MITgcmutils.netcdf.netcdf_variable.shape
documentation:
Returns the shape tuple of the data variable.

This is a read-only attribute and can not be modified in the
same manner of other numpy arrays.

methods.name: MITgcmutils.netcdf.netcdf_variable.getValue
documentation:
Retrieve a scalar value from a `netcdf_variable` of length one.

Raises
------
ValueError
    If the netcdf variable is an array of length greater than one,
    this exception will be raised.

methods.name: MITgcmutils.netcdf.netcdf_variable.assignValue
documentation:
Assign a scalar value to a `netcdf_variable` of length one.

Parameters
----------
value : scalar
    Scalar value (of compatible type) to assign to a length-one netcdf
    variable. This value will be written to file.

Raises
------
ValueError
    If the input is not a scalar, or if the destination is not a length-one
    netcdf variable.

methods.name: MITgcmutils.netcdf.netcdf_variable.typecode
documentation:
Return the typecode of the variable.

Returns
-------
typecode : char
    The character typecode of the variable (eg, 'i' for int).

methods.name: MITgcmutils.netcdf.netcdf_variable.itemsize
documentation:
Return the itemsize of the variable.

Returns
-------
itemsize : int
    The element size of the variable (eg, 8 for float64).

A data object for the `netcdf` module.

`netcdf_variable` objects are constructed by calling the method
`netcdf_file.createVariable` on the `netcdf_file` object. `netcdf_variable`
objects behave much like array objects defined in numpy, except that their
data resides in a file. Data is read by indexing and written by assigning
to an indexed subset; the entire array can be accessed by the index `[:]`
or (for scalars) by using the methods `getValue` and `assignValue`.
`netcdf_variable` objects also have attribute `shape` with the same meaning
as for arrays, but the shape cannot be modified. There is another read-only
attribute `dimensions`, whose value is the tuple of dimension names.

All other attributes correspond to variable attributes defined in
the NetCDF file. Variable attributes are created by assigning to an
attribute of the `netcdf_variable` object.

# Parameters

data

: The data array that holds the values for the variable.
  Typically, this is initialized as empty, but with the proper shape.

typecode

: Desired data-type for the data array.

size

: Desired element size for the data array.

shape

: The shape of the array. This should match the lengths of the
  variable's dimensions.

dimensions

: The names of the dimensions used by the variable. Must be in the
  same order of the dimension lengths given by `shape`.

attributes

: Attribute values (any type) keyed by string names. These attributes
  become attributes for the netcdf_variable object.

# Attributes

dimensions

: List of names of dimensions used by the variable object.

isrec, shape

: Properties

# See also

isrec, shape

functions.name: MITgcmutils.mnc.rdmnc
documentation:
Read one or more variables from an mnc file set.

# Parameters

fpatt

: glob pattern for netcdf files comprising the set

varnames

: list of variables to read (default all)

iters

: list of iterations (not time) to read

slices

: tuple of slices to read from each variable
  (typically given as numpy.s\_[...])

# Returns

dict of numpy arrays

: dictionary of variable arrays

# Example

```pycon
>>> S = rdmnc("mnc_*/state.0000000000.*', ['U', 'V'], slices=numpy.s_[..., 10:-10, 10:-10])
>>> u = S['U']
>>> v = S['V']
```

# Notes

Can currently read only one file set (i.e., 1 file per tile),
not several files split in time.

Consider using mnc_files for more control (and similar convenience).
The same restriction about multiple files applies, however.

classes.name: MITgcmutils.mnc.MNC

methods.name: MITgcmutils.mnc.MNC.close
documentation: Close tile files

A file object for MNC (tiled NetCDF) data.

Should behave mostly like scipy.io.netcdf.netcdf_file in 'r' mode.

# Parameters

fpatt

: glob pattern for tile files

layout

: which global layout to use:

  'model'

  : use layout implied by Nx, Ny

  'exch2'

  : use exch2 global layout

  'faces'

  : variables are lists of exch2 faces

  default is to use exch2 layout if present, model otherwise

# Example

```pycon
>>> nc = mnc_files('mnc_*/state.0000000000.t*.nc')
>>> temp = nc.variables['Temp'][:]
>>> salt = nv.variables['S'][:]
>>> nc.close()
temp and salt are now assembled (global) arrays of shape (Nt, Nr, Ny, Nx)
where Nt is the number iterations found in the file (in this case probably 1).
```

# Notes

The multitime option is not implemented, i.e., MNC cannot read files split
in time.

functions.name: MITgcmutils.llc.llc.contourf
documentation:
Create a contourf plot of a 2-D llc array (with tricontour).

Call signatures:

```
contourf(X, Y, C, N, **kwargs)

contourf(X, Y, C, V, **kwargs)
```

# Parameters

X

: x coordinates of the grid points

Y

: y coordinates of the grid points

C

: array of color values.

N

: number of levels

V

: list of levels

kwargs

: passed to tricontour.

functions.name: MITgcmutils.llc.llc.contour

Create a contour plot of a 2-D llc array (with tricontour).

Call signatures:

```
contour(X, Y, C, N, **kwargs)

contour(X, Y, C, V, **kwargs)
```

# Parameters

X

: x coordinates of the grid points

Y

: y coordinates of the grid points

C

: array of color values.

N

: number of levels

V

: list of levels

kwargs

: passed to tricontour.

convert global 'flat' field into mds data;
only fields with 2 to 5 dimensions are allowed

functions.name: MITgcmutils.llc.llc.faces
documentation: convert mds multidimensional data into a list with 6 faces

convert 6 faces to mds 2D data,
inverse opertation of llc.faces

functions.name: MITgcmutils.llc.llc._faces2D
documentation: convert mds 2D data into a list with 6 faces

functions.name: MITgcmutils.llc.llc.pcol
documentation:
Create a pseudo-color plot of a 2-D llc array (with plt.pcolormesh).

Call signatures:

```
pcol(X, Y, C, **kwargs)

pcol(X, Y, C, m, **kwargs)
```

# Parameters

X

: x coordinates of the grid point corners (G-points)

Y

: y coordinates of the grid point corners (G-points)

C

: array of color values.

m

: map projection to use.
  NOTE: currently not all projections work

kwargs

: passed to plt.pcolormesh.

functions.name: MITgcmutils.llc.llc._getDims
documentation: Retrieve dimensions of input fields and do some sanity checks

functions.name: MITgcmutils.llc.llc.div
documentation:
Compute divergence of vector field (U,V) on llc grid

Call signatures:

```
divergence = div(U, V, DXG, DYG, RAC, HFW, HFS)
divergence = div(U, V)
divergence = div(U, V, DXG, DYG)
divergence = div(U, V, DXG, DYG, RAC)
divergence = div(U, V, DXG, DYG, hfw=HFW, hfs=HFS)
```

# Parameters

u

: x-component of vector field at u-point

v

: y-component of vector field at v-point

dxg

: grid spacing in x across v-point, defaults to one

dyg

: grid spacing in y across u-point, defaults to one

rac

: grid cell area, defaults to dxg\*dyg

hfw

: hFac at u-point, defaults to one

hfs

: hFac at v-point, defaults to one

functions.name: MITgcmutils.llc.llc.uv2c
documentation:
Average vector component (u,v) to center points on llc grid

Call signatures:

```
uc,vc = uv2c(U,V)
```

# Parameters

U

: x-component of vector field at u-point

V

: y-component of vector field at v-point

functions.name: MITgcmutils.llc.llc.grad
documentation:
Compute horizontal gradient of scalar field X on llc grid

Call signatures:

```
dXdx, dXdy = div(X, DXC, DYC, HFW, HFS)
dXdx, dXdy = div(X)
dXdx, dXdy = div(X, DXC, DYC)
dXdx, dXdy = div(X, hfw=HFW, hfs=HFS)
```

# Parameters

X

: scalar field at c-point

dxc

: grid spacing in x across u-point, defaults to one

dyc

: grid spacing in y across v-point, defaults to one

hfw

: hFac at u-point, defaults to one

hfs

: hFac at v-point, defaults to one

functions.name: MITgcmutils.mds.rdmds
documentation:
Read meta-data files as written by MITgcm.

Call signatures:

> a = rdmds(fname,...)
>
> a,its,meta = rdmds(fname,...,returnmeta=True)

# Parameters

fname

: name of file to read, without the '.data' or '.meta' suffix. If itrs is
  given, the iteration number is added to `fname` as well. `fname` may
  contain shell wildcards, which is useful for tile files organized into
  directories, e.g.,

  > T = rdmds('prefix\*/T', 2880)

  will read prefix0000/T.0000002880.\*, prefix0001/T.0000002880.\*, ...
  (and any others that match the wildcard, so be careful how you name things!)

itrs

: Iteration number(s). With itrs=-1, will try to read

  > fname.meta or fname.001.001.meta, ...

  If itrs is a list of integers of an integer, it will read the corresponding

  > fname.000000iter.meta, ...

  If itrs is np.nan, it will read all iterations for which files are found.
  If itrs is np.inf, it will read the highest iteration found.

machineformat

: endianness ('b' or 'l', default 'b')

rec

: list of records to read (default all)
  useful for pickups and multi-field diagnostics files

fill_value

: fill value for missing (blank) tiles (default 0)

astype

: data type to return (default: double precision)
  None: keep data type/precision of file

region

: (x0,x1,y0,y1) read only this region (default (0,nx,0,ny))

lev

: list of levels to read, or, for multiple dimensions
  (excluding x,y), tuple(!) of lists (see examples below)

usememmap

: if True, use a memory map for reading data (default False)
  recommended when using lev, or region with global files
  to save memory and, possibly, time

# Returns

a

: numpy array of the data read

its

: list of iteration numbers read (only if returnmeta=True)

meta

: dictionary of metadata (only if returnmeta=True)

# Examples

```pycon
>>> XC = rdmds('XC')
>>> XC = rdmds('res_*/XC')
>>> T = rdmds('T.0000002880')
>>> T = rdmds('T',2880)
>>> T2 = rdmds('T',[2880,5760])
>>> T,its = rdmds('T',numpy.inf)
>>> VVEL = rdmds('pickup',2880,rec=range(50,100))
>>> a5 = rdmds('diags',2880,rec=0,lev=[5])
>>> a = rdmds('diags',2880,rec=0,lev=([0],[0,1,5,6,7]))
>>> from numpy import r_
>>> a = rdmds('diags',2880,rec=0,lev=([0],r_[:2,5:8]))  # same as previous
>>> a = rdmds('diags',2880,rec=0)[0, [0,1,5,6,7], ...]  # same, but less efficient
>>> a = rdmds('diags',2880)[0, 0, [0,1,5,6,7], ...]     # even less efficient
```

functions.name: MITgcmutils.mds.wrmds
documentation:
Write an array to an mds meta/data file set.

If itr is given, the files will be named fbase.0000000itr.data and
fbase.0000000itr.meta, otherwise just fbase.data and fbase.meta.

Parameters
----------
fbase : string
    Name of file to write, without the '.data' or '.meta' suffixes,
    and without the iteration number if itr is give
arr : array_like
    Numpy array to write
itr : int or None
    If given, this iteration number will be appended to the file name
dataprec : string
    precision of resulting file ('float32' or 'float64')
ndims : int
    number of non-record dimensions; extra (leading) dimensions
    will be folded into 1 record dimension
nrecords : int
    number of records; will fold as many leading dimensions as
    necessary (has to match shape!)
times : float or list of floats
    times to write into meta file.  Either a single float or a list
    of two for a time interval
fields : list of strings
    list of fields
simulation : string
    string describing the simulation
machineformat : string
    'b' or 'l' for big or little endian
deltat : float
    time step; provide in place of either times or itr to have one
    computed from the other
dimlist : tuple
    dimensions as will be stored in file (only useful when passing
    meta data from an existing file to wrmds as keyword args)
